explanation: A database is by definition an organized collection of interrelated records or data. A spreadsheet is a tool for cells and formulas, a text file has no record structure, and an MIS is a system built on top of a database rather than the data collection itself.

In a database (the wording follows MS Access terminology) a query is saved as a persistent object in the database and can be re-run later. A filter is transient: it applies only to the current view of the data and is discarded when you close it.

Sequential file organization stores records physically in ascending or descending order of a key field, so reading the file walks the keys in order. The trap is direct (random) access, which computes a record address by hashing or indexing and therefore imposes no ordering on the stored records.

The stem is poorly worded, but of the four options only 'database created using a DBMS package' is actually a database. Word documents and Excel worksheets are file formats, not DBMS-managed databases, so 'All of the above' is also wrong.

DML covers manipulating the data itself: INSERT, DELETE, UPDATE and SELECT, which are options B, C and D. Creating a table is DDL (CREATE), not DML, so it is the one thing in the list DML is NOT used for. Note the negative stem: the correct answer is the odd one out.

A superkey is any attribute set that uniquely identifies a row; a candidate key is a superkey with no removable attribute, that is, a minimal superkey. The classic trap is 'primary key': the primary key is just the one candidate key the designer picks, so minimality defines candidate keys, not primary keys.

A query nested inside the WHERE or HAVING clause of another query is a subquery (nested query); the inner query is evaluated to supply values the outer query filters on. 'Super query', 'master query' and 'multi-query' are not SQL terms.

A data dictionary stores metadata about the data: table and column names, data types, constraints, and where data is located. The physical capacity of the disk storage device is a hardware property, not metadata about the data, so it is the item the dictionary does not provide. Options C and D are garbled fragments that do not describe a data dictionary at all.

The data hierarchy runs field, record, file, database, each level containing the one below it. A field (for example a single album title) is the smallest of these that is processed as one unit; a record is a group of fields, so it is larger, not smaller.

A report generator takes data from the database and formats it for printed or on-screen output. Updating files is done by DML or a data entry form, and data entry is the operator's job, so neither is a report generator's function.

Related fields grouped together form one record (for example the title, artist and year fields of one album). Going one level up, related records grouped together form a data file, which is why option A is a level too high.

DROP is the SQL DDL verb for removing a schema object, so DROP FUNCTION removes a stored function. The trap is DELETE, which is DML and removes rows from a table; it cannot remove the function itself. REMOVE and ERASE are not SQL keywords.

SELECT DISTINCT is the standard SQL clause that collapses duplicate rows so each distinct set of values is returned once. SELECT UNIQUE is only a legacy Oracle synonym and is not standard SQL, which is why 'All of the above' is wrong.

ALTER TRIGGER trigger_name ENABLE / DISABLE is the command that turns an individual trigger on or off. ALTER TABLE can enable or disable all triggers on a table at once in some dialects, but it is not the command that targets a trigger, and MODIFY TRIGGER does not exist.

Modifying a database means changing the stored data: insertion, deletion and updating. Sorting only changes the order in which rows are presented in a result set; the stored data is untouched, so it is not a modification.
